APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses a classic and timely NLP/ML problem—low-resource POS tagging with noisy cross-lingual projection—and proposes a novel noise-handling layer for a BiLSTM, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology and evaluation follow community standards (comparisons to strong baselines including Duong et al. (2014), experiments on CoNLL languages, Europarl projection data, and real low-resource languages Malagasy and Kinyarwanda), and the paper is positioned appropriately for the venue’s audience interested in distant supervision and cross-lingual learning.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion), and its main contributions are stated clearly up front (novel noise layer for BiLSTM POS tagging and joint learning from gold and projected annotations). The writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, but a few presentation details are missing in the summary (notation and figures/tables are not described, and hyperparameters are not reported), suggesting minor clarity/presentation gaps rather than major issues.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by experiments across eight simulated low-resource settings and two real languages (Madagascar, Kinyarwanda), and the method is compared against appropriate baselines including the Duong et al. (2014a) state-of-the-art and several BiLSTM and MaxEnt variants. No mathematical derivations or proofs are needed for the proposed neural architecture and none are reported, which is acceptable here. However, experimental reporting omits hyperparameter details and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which weakens confidence in the empirical claims. Overall the conclusions are plausible and largely supported but the missing experimental details are a notable shortcoming.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive — evaluation on eight simulated low-resource settings and two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda) with comparisons to a prior state-of-the-art (Duong et al., 2014a) and several strong baselines supports the main claims. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations of cross-lingual projection are acknowledged. However, the paper appears to lack reported hyperparameters, code release, and explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond baseline comparisons, which weakens reproducibility and deeper understanding of design choices.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of low-resource POS tagging by explicitly modeling noise in cross-lingual projection, proposing a BiLSTM tagger with a noise layer that jointly learns from small gold corpora and larger noisy projections. It matches or exceeds state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings and outperforms prior work on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy and Kinyarwanda), suggesting practical effectiveness. The approach is generalizable to other noisy supervision sources (distant supervision, crowdsourcing), so it is likely to be built upon and to open meaningful follow-up research, though it is not likely to be fully transformative across NLP by itself.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate baselines including the prior state-of-the-art (Duong et al., 2014a), several MaxEnt and BiLSTM variants, and evaluates on the same datasets (CoNLL for simulated settings and Garrette & Baldridge for real low-resource languages) using POS tagging accuracy. It reports matching or exceeding prior work on eight simulated settings and outperforming prior work on two real languages, indicating fair head-to-head comparisons and acknowledgement when prior methods are competitive. One minor concern is that hyperparameters were not reported (per the summary), which could affect reproducibility/fairness, but overall the set of baselines and reported comparisons appear comprehensive with only minor omissions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new component — an explicit noise layer on top of a BiLSTM tagger that models and corrects errors from cross-lingual projection while jointly learning from a small gold corpus and larger noisy projected annotations. It is differentiated from prior work (e.g., Duong et al. 2014a) and is empirically validated by equaling or exceeding state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings and outperforming prior work on Malagasy and Kinyarwanda, indicating a non-trivial methodological extension. This approach would likely inform experts working on distant supervision and low-resource POS tagging, though it builds on existing projection and neural tagging paradigms rather than proposing a wholly new problem formulation.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Important methodological components are described (BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer, use of one-to-one GIZA++ alignments and filtered parallel sentences) and the datasets (Europarl, CoNLL, Garrette & Baldridge) and evaluation metric (POS tagging accuracy) are publicly known. However, hyperparameters and detailed model/training configurations are explicitly not reported, and code availability is not mentioned, making exact replication difficult. Overall the paper can likely be reimplemented from the high-level description but some important details needed for exact reproducibility are absent.

main_claims: Introduces a novel sequence tagging approach that learns to correct errors from cross-lingual projection using an explicit noise layer.
Proposes a BiLSTM-based POS tagger with a noise-handling layer to jointly learn from a small gold-annotated corpus and noisy projected annotations.
The method equals or exceeds state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings and outperforms prior work on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy and Kinyarwanda).
The technique is general and may be useful for exploiting other noisy annotations such as distant supervision and crowd-sourced annotations.
_summary.methodology: Train a bidirectional LSTM POS tagger with an explicit noise layer to model and correct noisy projected tags, jointly learning from a small gold-annotated dataset and larger cross-lingually projected annotations (using one-to-one GIZA++ alignments and filtered parallel sentences).
datasets: Europarl (parallel data, 200k sentences per Euro language)
CoNLL data (Buchholz and Marsi, 2006) for annotated data in eight languages
Garrette and Baldridge (2013) datasets for Malagasy and Kinyarwanda (real-world low-resource experiments)
Parallel corpora between target languages and English (used for projection)
baselines: Supervised maximum entropy model trained on annotated data (reported baseline)
BiLSTM Annotated (BiLSTM trained on annotated data)
BiLSTM Projected (BiLSTM trained on projected data)
BiLSTM Ann+Proj (BiLSTM trained on union of annotated and projected data)
limitations: Directly projected labels are very noisy and unwise to use without correction.
Cross-lingual projection suffers from noise due to poor word alignments and cross-lingual syntactic divergence.
Gold annotated data is expensive and typically available only in small quantities for low-resource languages.
